content.full_text:
January 10, 1991

TO: City Council

Re: Campaign Contribution Limits in Municipal Election - Transfers

Proposition 73 prohibited transfers of funds to (1) another campaign committee of the same candidate (including reelection to the same office or another office), and (2) another candidate for public office. The Court has invalidated much of Proposition 73, including these transfer prohibition provisions. This has been confirmed with the FPPC.

The invalidation of the transfer prohibitions was not made on the ground that they were intrinsically unconstitutional in themselves, but because they were tied into the fiscal year basis for the campaign contribution limits (which was the principal basis for the court's ruling). This is an important point, because it leaves discretion for the City to regulate or not regulate such transfers as part of its ordinance limiting campaign contributions.

The city's campaign contribution ordinance was first adopted in the mid 1970's, and was rewritten and readopted in 1987, before Proposition 73 was passed. The principal regulation of the City's ordinance is to impose a $249 contribution limit. It does not, however, directly address the question of transfers and therefore requires interpretation.

It is the interpretation of the City Clerk and City Attorney that the City's ordinance affects transfers as follows: (1) There is no limitation on transfers from a council candidate's campaign funds to another campaign fund of the same person (whether for reelection to the same office or any other public office); (2) The ordinance allows a transfer (contribution) from a council candidate's campaign fund to another council candidate's campaign committee, limited, however, to the $249 contribution limit on the same basis as other contributions.

The City Clerk will inform all candidates who file for the City's municipal election in November, of the transfer limitations of the City's campaign contribution ordinance.

Sharon Whitten
City Clerk

Allen Sprague
